pathway_title: Adult dizziness in primary care: threshold-led management and referral
scope: Adults in primary care with dizziness/vertigo presentations.
core_principle: Triage first for stroke/central red flags; otherwise manage likely peripheral vertigo in primary care using Hallpike/Epley and time-limited symptomatic treatment.

immediate_same_day_emergency: Sudden-onset dizziness/vertigo with focal neurological deficit (including new unsteadiness, central-pattern nystagmus, new deafness) -> emergency stroke pathway.
Acute vestibular syndrome where BPPV/postural hypotension do not explain symptoms and no trained HINTS assessor available -> emergency stroke pathway.
Any vertigo with major neurological red flags (diplopia, dysarthria/speech disturbance, limb weakness/numbness, severe new headache, reduced consciousness).
urgent_or_soon_primary_care_review: Persistent severe vomiting/dehydration, inability to mobilise safely, or diagnostic uncertainty requiring early reassessment.
New hearing loss/tinnitus with persistent vertigo where peripheral diagnosis unclear (local ENT/acute pathway per board protocol).
routine_referral: BPPV not resolving after appropriate repositioning manoeuvres -> routine Audiology/vestibular physiotherapy/ENT (local pathway).
Ongoing vertigo/balance symptoms beyond ~6 weeks despite initial management -> vestibular rehab or ENT.
Suspected vestibular migraine or Meniere-type recurrent episodes not controlled in primary care -> routine specialty referral per local pathway.
manage_in_primary_care: Transient positional rotational vertigo with positive Hallpike consistent with BPPV -> Epley/canalith repositioning.
No focal neurological deficit and low-risk profile -> focused assessment, safety-netting, early mobilisation advice.
Avoid prolonged vestibular suppressants; restrict symptomatic drugs to short acute phase only.

minimum_primary_care_dataset: Clarify symptom phenotype: vertigo vs presyncope vs disequilibrium/light-headedness
Onset, duration, triggers (positional/head movement), recurrence pattern
Neurological symptoms/signs including gait and eye movements
Otological symptoms: hearing loss, tinnitus, aural fullness
Hallpike when indicated and clinician trained
safety_net: Escalate immediately if any focal neurology, severe new headache, speech/visual deficit, new unilateral weakness/sensory loss.
Advise return if worsening, persistent inability to function, falls risk, new hearing loss, or no improvement after expected time-course.

evidence_confidence.rationale: Strong threshold statements from NICE NG127 and concordant Scottish pathway content (Right Decisions, territorial boards). Confidence reduced by inability to directly extract full NICE CKS content and absence of a current dedicated SIGN dizziness guideline.
